description: Geb, also known as Seb or Keb, is the Egyptian god of the Earth. In Egyptian mythology, Geb is the son of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture), and the husband and brother of Nut (the sky). According to the creation myth, Geb and Nut were initially locked in a close embrace, preventing creation from occurring. Shu separated them, pushing Nut upwards to become the sky and leaving Geb below as the Earth. This separation is a central motif in Egyptian cosmology, explaining the structure of the world. Geb is often depicted as a man lying beneath Nut, sometimes green-skinned to represent vegetation, and occasionally with a goose on his head, symbolizing his connection to the primordial egg from which the sun emerged. As the god of the Earth, Geb was responsible for the fertility of the land, the growth of crops, and the provision of minerals and resources. He was also considered a wise and just ruler, and in some myths, he is said to have ruled Egypt before the pharaohs. He is associated with snakes and is sometimes depicted with a snake on his head. After his rule on Earth, he passed the kingship to Osiris. He plays a crucial role in the afterlife, as the deceased are buried within him, returning to the earth from which they came. Geb's role highlights the Egyptian reverence for the Earth as the source of life and sustenance.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Geb is the literal embodiment of the Earth.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Represents the fertility and vegetation of the Earth.

associations.name: Goose
associations.significance: The Great Cackler, believed to have laid the cosmic egg containing the sun.

associations.name: Fertility
associations.significance: As the Earth, Geb is the source of all life and abundance.

associations.name: Stability
associations.significance: Represents the solid ground and foundation of existence.

associations.name: Plants
associations.significance: All plant life springs from Geb's body.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and precious materials were made to Geb to ensure the continued fertility of the land and a bountiful harvest.

festivals.name: Harvest Festivals
festivals.description: While no festival was exclusively for Geb, he was celebrated during harvest festivals, with thanks given for the earth's bounty.

cultural.modernLegacy: Geb's symbolism persists in modern neo-pagan and Kemetic reconstructionist movements, where he is revered as a source of earthly power and stability. He is also referenced in some modern literature and art inspired by Egyptian mythology.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, according to Egyptian cosmology, there was only Nun, the primordial waters of chaos. From Nun emerged Atum, the self-created deity. Atum then created Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture). Shu and Tefnut, in turn, gave birth to Geb (Earth) and Nut (sky). Initially, Geb and Nut were locked in an eternal embrace, preventing any form of creation or the passage of time. This close union was seen as a problem by Atum, who tasked Shu with separating the two siblings. Shu, the god of air, stood between Geb and Nut, lifting Nut upwards to form the sky and pinning Geb down to become the Earth. This separation was a violent act, and it is said that Nut still yearns for Geb, causing rain to fall upon the Earth. Geb's birth and subsequent separation from Nut are fundamental to the Egyptian understanding of the world's structure and the forces that govern it.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most significant myths involving Geb is the story of the separation of Geb and Nut by Shu. This myth explains the creation of the world as the Egyptians understood it. Another important myth involves Geb's role in the succession of the Egyptian throne. After Ra, the sun god, ruled the Earth, he passed the kingship to Geb. Geb then ruled wisely and justly, bringing prosperity to the land. Later, Geb passed the throne to Osiris, his son, marking the beginning of a new era in Egyptian history. When Osiris was murdered by his brother Set, Geb mourned deeply and played a role in the subsequent conflict between Set and Horus, Osiris's son. Geb ultimately sided with Horus, recognizing him as the rightful heir to the throne. This decision solidified Horus's position as the divine ruler of Egypt and established the pharaohs as his earthly representatives. Geb's involvement in these myths highlights his importance as a ruler, a source of stability, and a key figure in the divine order of the cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Geb did not have temples dedicated solely to his worship, he was revered as a fundamental aspect of the natural world. His presence was acknowledged in temples dedicated to other deities, and offerings were made to him to ensure the fertility of the land and a bountiful harvest. As the Earth itself, Geb was omnipresent and accessible to all Egyptians. Farmers would invoke his name when planting crops, and prayers were offered to him to protect against natural disasters such as droughts and earthquakes. The Egyptians believed that Geb's well-being was directly linked to their own prosperity and survival. Therefore, they treated the Earth with respect and reverence, recognizing its vital role in their lives. Although no specific rituals were exclusively devoted to Geb, his importance was deeply ingrained in the daily lives and religious practices of the ancient Egyptians. His role underscores the Egyptian reverence for the natural world and its life-sustaining properties.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Geb's legacy extends beyond ancient Egypt, influencing later cultures and belief systems. His role as the Earth god resonates with similar deities found in other mythologies, such as Gaia in Greek mythology and Terra in Roman mythology. In modern times, Geb continues to be recognized and revered in neo-pagan and Kemetic reconstructionist movements. These groups seek to revive and practice ancient Egyptian religious beliefs and rituals, often incorporating Geb into their pantheon of deities. He is seen as a symbol of earthly power, stability, and fertility. Furthermore, Geb's image and mythology have inspired artists, writers, and filmmakers, who draw upon his story to explore themes of creation, nature, and the interconnectedness of all things. His enduring presence in popular culture and religious practices demonstrates the lasting impact of Egyptian mythology and the enduring appeal of the Earth god Geb.

shortDescription: Geb is the Egyptian god of the Earth, representing the ground we stand on and the source of all life and vegetation.
